coredata.dc:title: Photoelectric balance of rear electrode in bifacial perovskite solar cells: Construction of 0D/1D/2D composite electrode based on silver nanowires to boost photovoltaic output
coredata.dc:creator: Lü, Wanhong
Fan, Lin
Wang, Mingyue
Wang, Pengfei
Sun, Qinghua
Yang, Shuo
Han, Donglai
Wang, Fengyou
Liu, Huilian
Yang, Jinghai
Yang, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectric imbalance problems of rear transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) limit the photovoltaic (PV) performance of bifacial planar heterojunction perovskite solar cells (PH–PSCs). We exploit the superior electrical advantages of 0-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (C QDs), 1-dimensional (1D) silver nanowires (Ag NWs), and 2-dimensional (2D) ultrathin Ag films to develop a novel rear TCE with ideal photoelectric balance, i.e., Ag NW + C QD/Ag NW/molybdenum oxide (MoOx)/ultrathin Ag (denoted as A + C/A/M/A) rear TCE with a 0D/1D/2D composite structure. We show that C QDs fully fill the voids within the Ag NW network, which effectively accelerates the charge transfer/collection, thereby enhancing the PV output of PH-PSCs. Moreover, the film quality and photoelectric properties of the rear TCEs are further improved by depositing the MoOx/ultrathin Ag films onto the A + C composite network, which significantly improves PV performance. More importantly, MoOx is key to realizing the photoelectric balance of rear TCEs because it not only has excellent antireflection effects, but also promotes the formation of high-quality ultrathin Ag film. As a result, the efficiencies of the A + C/A/M/A-based PH-PSC under front and rear illuminations are 16.09% and 12.59%, respectively, which are superior to that of most reported Ag NW-based PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bifacial perovskite solar cells
Transparent conductive electrode
Silver nanowire network
Photoelectric balance
Charge dynamics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) J–V curves of PH-PSCs fabricated with A-4, A-5, and A-6. Surface SEM images of different Ag NW networks: (b) A-4, (c) A-5, and (d) A-6. (e) Schematic of contact between cylindrical Ag NW and planar substrate. (f) Transmittance (550 nm) plotted as a function of the sheet resistance for the three Ag NW networks. (g) Haze factors of the A-4, A-5, and A-6 films coated on ITO-glass. (h) Figure of merit values of corresponding Ag NW networks.

(a) Schematic of the manufacturing process of different Ag NW + C QD composite networks. (b)–(c) Surface SEM images and elemental mappings (Ag–La and C-Ka) of samples without and with C QDs. (d) Sheet resistances and (e) transmittance spectra of different TCEs coated on ITO-glass substrates. (f) Typical J–V curves of PH-PSCs measured in reverse scan with a scan rate of 130 mV s−1 under simulated AM 1.5G one sun illumination (100 mW cm−2).

Statistical (a) efficiency, (b) J SC, (c) V OC, and (d) FF of 30 PH-PSCs based on the A-5, A + C-3/A-2, and A + C/A/M/A rear TCEs prepared under the same conditions. Top-view SEM images of different samples measured under an accelerating voltage of 5 kV: (e) ITO/A-5, (f) ITO/A + C-3/A-2, and (g) ITO/A + C/A/M/A. (h) Steady-state PL spectra of the glass/perovskite/HTL/TCE substrates fabricated with the above three TCEs. (i) Nyquist plots and (j) dark-current measurements of PH-PSCs fabricated with the A-5, A + C-3/A-2, and A + C/A/M/A rear TCEs. The inset of (i) depicts the equivalent circuit model of the fitted EIS.

(a) Photographs and PV test model of the bifacial PH-PSC. (b) J–V curves of the champion device illuminated on different sides (inset: detailed PV parameters). (c) Corresponding EQE spectra and integrated J SC. All J–V characteristics were measured in reverse scan with a scan rate of 130 mV s−1 under AM 1.5G illumination.
Summary of PV parameters, rear electrode configurations, and key technologies of devices based on previously reported Ag NW TCEs.

row.entry: Ag NWs embedded in rGO layer
Ag NWs + C QDs/Ag NWs/MoOx/ultrathin Ag
Solution spin coating and thermal evaporation
Solution spin coating and thermal evaporation

head.ce:title: Photoelectric balance of rear electrode in bifacial perovskite solar cells: Construction of 0D/1D/2D composite electrode based on silver nanowires to boost photovoltaic output

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Jilin Normal University, Changchun, 130103, China.
Corresponding author. Jilin Normal University, Changchun, 130103, China.
Corresponding author. Jilin Normal University, Changchun, 130103, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectric imbalance problems of rear transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) limit the photovoltaic (PV) performance of bifacial planar heterojunction perovskite solar cells (PH–PSCs). We exploit the superior electrical advantages of 0-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (C QDs), 1-dimensional (1D) silver nanowires (Ag NWs), and 2-dimensional (2D) ultrathin Ag films to develop a novel rear TCE with ideal photoelectric balance, i.e., Ag NW + C QD/Ag NW/molybdenum oxide (MoOx)/ultrathin Ag (denoted as A + C/A/M/A) rear TCE with a 0D/1D/2D composite structure. We show that C QDs fully fill the voids within the Ag NW network, which effectively accelerates the charge transfer/collection, thereby enhancing the PV output of PH-PSCs. Moreover, the film quality and photoelectric properties of the rear TCEs are further improved by depositing the MoOx/ultrathin Ag films onto the A + C composite network, which significantly improves PV performance. More importantly, MoOx is key to realizing the photoelectric balance of rear TCEs because it not only has excellent antireflection effects, but also promotes the formation of high-quality ultrathin Ag film. As a result, the efficiencies of the A + C/A/M/A-based PH-PSC under front and rear illuminations are 16.09% and 12.59%, respectively, which are superior to that of most reported Ag NW-based PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Bifacial planar heterojunction perovskite solar cells (PH–PSCs) not only inherit the advantages of conventional PH-PSCs but also demonstrate superior cost effectiveness and unparalleled commercial competitiveness owing to their unique backlight harvesting capabilities [1,2]. The development of efficient and stable bifacial PH-PSCs is of great significance for promoting market applications such as perovskite-based tandem devices, semitransparent building components (windows/roofs), and flexible/wearable electronics [3–5]. Front and rear transparent conductive electrodes (TCEs) with high transparency and low resistivity are key to achieving bidirectional photoelectric conversion of solar cells [6]. In recent years, indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass substrates have been extensively used as front TCEs of solar cells owing to their excellent photoelectric properties [2,7–9], and the photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of conventional PH-PSCs is up to 25.5% [10]. However, owing to the processing constraints of rear TCEs (i.e., low-temperature deposition, no electron beam bombardment, and no solvent etching), it is difficult to prepare high-quality ITO TCEs on top of perovskites by using conventional ITO deposition techniques (vacuum evaporation and/or magnetron sputtering) [11,12]. To date, bifacial PSCs based on ITO rear TCEs commonly exhibit different degrees of optical and electrical losses; in particular, the photoelectric imbalance of ITO is common under low-temperature sputtering [13,14].

Thus, to develop efficient bifacial PH-PSCs, various rear TCE materials, such as metal nanowire [15], conductive polymers [16], carbon nanotubes [17], and graphenes [18], have been extensively investigated as alternatives to ITO. Among these materials, 1-dimensional (1D) silver nanowires (Ag NWs) are one of the most promising candidates owing to their outstanding transmittance (∼90% at 550 nm), low sheet resistance (<20 Ω sq−1), and excellent flexibility [1,19,20]. In recent years, rear TCEs composed of random Ag NW networks have been prepared by scalable spin coating/spray coating, transfer, and/or lamination [21–24], and the resulting flexible, bifacial/semitransparent, and tandem devices exhibit greatly improved performance [1,5,21]. Compared with the transfer and lamination procedures, which are difficult to operate and costly to prepare, solution spin-coating technology has attracted extensive attention owing to its advantages of simplicity, low cost, and high throughput [1,22]. However, a series of electrical losses caused by the Ag NW network structure are the main reasons for the optoelectronic imbalance of rear TCEs and the degradation of photovoltaic (PV) performance. For instance, 1) the adhesion between the Ag NWs and adjacent contacts is poor; 2) the limited contact area cannot effectively collect the carriers generated at the p-n junction; and 3) local melting occurs in the Ag NW network, resulting in a sharp increase in sheet resistance [1,25].

Recently, research aimed at addressing the above problems has received increasing attention [1,3,4,15,22,25–29]. Khoa et al. used a simple spin-coating method to incorporate a fullerene, C60, into the Ag NW network, effectively improving the interface contact and energy level alignment [15]. Han et al. constructed a ZnO/Ag film/ZnO multilayer electrode; the outer ZnO layers enclosing the Ag NWs in a sandwich structure prevented local melting-induced disconnection of the Ag NWs at the cross junctions, which enhanced the thermal stability of the Ag NWs and accelerated charge-carrier injection/collection [26]. Therefore, TCEs comprising Ag NW based composites prevent the occurrence of empty space (unoccupied by Ag NWs), which improves adhesion, inhibits local melting, and promotes effective carrier dynamics. Therefore, predictably, combining 1D Ag NWs with the advantages of 0-dimensional quantum dots (0D QDs) and 2-dimensional (2D) metal/metal oxide thin layers to develop Ag NW-based 0D/1D/2D composite TCEs is of great significance to achieve photoelectric balance of rear TCEs and more efficient bifacial PH-PSCs [30,31].

In this work, we exploited the electrical advantages of 0D carbon quantum dots (C QDs) and 2D ultrathin Ag films to design and construct a novel Ag NW + C QD/Ag NW/molybdenum oxide (MoOx)/ultrathin Ag (denoted herein as A + C/A/M/A) rear TCE with a 0D/1D/2D composite structure to achieve ideal photoelectric balance. The morphology and photoelectric properties of each component in the composite system were systematically regulated by continuous spin coating and thermal evaporation, and the effects of different TCE structures on charge dynamics and PV parameters in the PH-PSCs (and/or bifacial devices) were clarified. The results indicated that 0D C QDs fully filled the voids among the 1D Ag NW network, effectively accelerating the charge transfer/collection rate, thus improving the PV performance of PH-PSCs. Notably, the A + C composite TCEs coated with Ag NWs exhibited better transmittance than the sample with exposed C QDs, which enhanced the backlight harvesting capability of bifacial PH-PSCs. Additionally, the deposition of MoOx/ultrathin Ag films on the A + C composite network further improved the film quality, interfacial contact, and photoelectric properties of the rear TCEs, which significantly enhanced PV output of PH-PSCs (especially bifacial devices). Meanwhile, MoOx film was key to achieving photoelectric balance of the rear TCEs, as it not only promoted the formation of high-quality 2D ultrathin Ag film but also showed excellent anti-reflection effect. As a result, the efficiencies of the A + C/A/M/A-based bifacial PH-PSC were up to 16.09% and 12.59% under front and rear illuminations, which was superior to that of most reported Ag NW-based PSCs. The 0D/1D/2D composite TCEs created in this work at low temperatures (≤150 °C) have great advantages for the development of highly efficient bifacial PH-PSCs, flexible cells, and monolithic tandem devices. Furthermore, the strategy presented herein represents a new approach for improving the photoelectric balance of rear TCEs.

Fig. S1a displays the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of the commercial Ag NW dispersion (1.25 wt% in isopropanol); a Ag NW absorption peak was observed in the region of 300–500 nm [32]. A photograph of the corresponding dispersion is shown in the inset. To clarify the optical properties of the Ag NW rear TCE, the Ag NW dispersion was spin coated onto a commercial ITO-glass substrate at 1000 rpm for 30 s. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the corresponding sample is shown in the inset of Fig. S1b. As shown in Fig. S1b, the highest transmittance recorded for the Ag NW network in the wavelength range of 300–1000 nm was only 40%, which may be attributed to the absorption, reflection and/or scattering effects of the relatively dense Ag NW layer [21,22]. Accordingly, to enhance the transmittance of the Ag NW rear TCE, a Ag NW solution diluted in isopropanol (IPA, 0.25 wt% Ag NWs in IPA) was continuously spin coated onto the substrates to obtain Ag NW networks with different thicknesses and densities. The resulting films were denoted according to the delivered number of spin-coating cycles: A-4 for four cycles, A-5 for five cycles, and A-6 for six cycles. Fig. S2 depicts the continuous spin-coating process (i.e., multiple cycles), and the specific deposition process is described in the Experimental Section.

The current density-voltage (J–V) curves of the PH-PSCs based on different Ag NW rear TCEs are presented in Fig. 1 a, and the corresponding PV parameters are listed in Table S1. The performance of the devices initially increased and then decreased with increasing Ag NW cycles, and the A-5-based PH-PSC exhibited the highest PV performance under AM 1.5G illumination (100 mA cm−2). Compared with the A-4-based device, the significant enhancement of PV parameters was attributed to the relatively dense A-5 (or/and A-6) rear TCE has more conduction paths for effective acceleration of carrier extraction/collection [18,21]. However, the overly dense A-6 network increased the junction resistance, thus deteriorating the PV output of the corresponding device [15]. The surface SEM images of A-4, A-5, and A-6 samples are displayed in Fig. 1b–d.

The correlation between the sheet resistance (Rs) and transmittance (at 550 nm) of the corresponding samples deposited on the ITO-glass substrates is described in Fig. 1f. Similar to the J-V characteristics, the A-5 sample exhibited the lowest Rs value (≈5.8 Ω sq−1), further confirming that the Ag NW network with the appropriate density and thickness can improve the charge dynamics and PV parameters of the PH-PSCs [27]. In contrast, the overly dense (or thick) Ag NW network (i.e., the A-6 sample) suppressed the PV output of the device because of its increased Rs [22]. Besides, from Fig. 1f, the transmittance (at 550 nm) of the Ag NW rear TCEs decreased exponentially with increasing Ag NW cycles. This was partly because of the enhanced absorption and/or reflectivity of Ag NW rising from the increase in network density and thickness [21,22]. Moreover, the haze factor of the samples also gradually strengthened with increasing Ag NW cycles, as depicted in Fig. 1g. This positive light-scattering effect is conducive to extending the length of the back-side light path, and thus improving the light collection and PV output of bifacial devices [33]. The total transmittance (TT) and vertical transmittance (VT) of the different Ag NW networks are shown in Fig. S3. All the samples exhibited excellent transmittance, and the haze factors were calculated using the following formula:
H a z e =
T T − V T
T T
× 100 %
[34].

To determine the optimal Ag NW rear TCE for bifacial PH-PSCs, the value of the figure of merits (FOMs) of the above three TCEs were calculated from the perspective of photoelectric balance [20]. As shown in Fig. 1h, FOM is defined as T10/R S, where T is the transmittance at 550 nm and R S is the sheet resistance [20]. Obviously, the A-5 sample delivered the highest FOM value among the three TCEs, which indicated a favorable balance between conductivity and transparency [20]. However, as shown in Fig. 1e and 1) the contact area between the Ag NW network and the hole transport layer (HTL) is limited, 2) voids exist within the Ag NW network, and 3) a considerable portion of nanowires are suspended in the random overlapping configuration. These all inhibit efficient charge transfer/collection, resulting in low PV performance [21]. Consequently, it was particularly important to further improve the electric properties of bare Ag NW rear TCEs for achieving efficient bifacial PH-PSCs.

The 0D C QDs with high charge mobility were selected as the filler of the bare Ag NW network to improve the contact characteristics and electrical properties of rear TCEs [15]. The Ag NW + C QD composite network, denoted as A + C CN, was obtained by continuously spin coating the C QD/Ag NW blend solution onto the planar HTL. After several cycles of A + C coating, the diluted Ag NW dispersion (0.25 wt%) was repeatedly spin coated onto the A + C CNs to obtain composite TCEs with different structures and photoelectric properties. The deposition process is shown in Fig. 2 a, and additional details are presented in the Experimental Section. The resulting samples were denoted as A-5 for the TCE without C QDs, A + C-1/A-4 for the TCE with one A + C cycle and four Ag NW cycles, A + C-3/A-2 for the TCE with three A + C cycles and two Ag NW cycles, and A + C-5 for the TCE with five A + C cycles.

The surface SEM images of the Ag NW networks without and with C QDs and the elemental mappings of Ag–La and C-Ka are shown in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. The incorporation of C QDs did not cause significant changes in the morphology of the Ag NW network. The uniform distribution of C QDs on the substrate surface (see Fig. 2c, C-Ka) indicated that the voids within the Ag NW network were well filled, effectively increasing the contact area between TCE and HTL, thus accelerating the charge transfer/collection [22]. The Rs changes of the different ITO-glass/A + C samples, shown in Fig. 2d, clearly indicated that the Rs values of the corresponding samples decreased with increasing A + C cycles, and the A + C-5 sample exhibited the best electrical properties. The corresponding J-V characteristics also show a similar tendency (as shown in Fig. S4), which further indicated that the filling of C QDs effectively improved the charge dynamics of Ag NW networks. However, as shown in Fig. 2e, the transmittance of the A + C-5 sample was significantly lower than that of the other three configurations because of light absorption by the exposed C QDs, which was expected to hinder backside light capture by the bifacial device [22,28]. Fortunately, the optical properties of the Ag NW-coated composite TCEs (i.e., A + C-1/A-4 and A + C-3/A-2) were not adversely affected by the C QDs. Notably, these samples exhibited up to 70% transmittance, which ensured sufficient light absorption by perovskite. These interesting results provide novel insights into the wide applications of C QDs in high-efficiency bifacial PV devices. Therefore, combined with the photoelectric advantages of TCEs, we considered the A + C-3/A-2 rear TCE more suitable for the preparation of efficient bifacial PH-PSCs. Fig. 2f presents the J-V curves of the PH-PSCs based on the above four rear TCEs, and the detailed PV parameters are summarized in Table S2. Because of the improved electrical properties of the rear TCEs by C QDs, the PV parameters of the planar devices based on the C QDs were significantly improved under AM 1.5G illumination (100 mW cm−2).

To further improve the PV output of the Ag NW-based PH-PSCs, MoOx/ultrathin Ag composite films were deposited on the A + C-3/A-2 rear TCE by thermal evaporation to form a novel rear TCE with a 0D/1D/2D composite structure, and its contribution to PV performance was analyzed in detail. The sample coated with MoOx/ultrathin Ag was denoted as A + C/A/M/A. The statistical PV parameters of the PH-PSCs based on the A-5, A + C-3/A-2, and A + C/A/M/A rear TCEs are shown in Fig. 3 a–d, and more detailed comparisons of the J-V parameters for the three devices are provided in Tables S3–S5 (Supporting Information). Thirty individual devices (10 cells based on A-5, 10 cells based on A + C-3/A-2, 10 cells based on A + C/A/M/A) were fabricated using the same process and measured under standard AM 1.5G illumination (100 mA cm−2). The PV parameters of the A + C/A/M/A-based devices were significantly higher than those of the other two solar cells, which mainly due to the MoOx/ultrathin Ag films improved the electrical properties of the whole TCEs. Fig. S5 shows the Rs changes of the different TCEs deposited on the ITO-glass substrates. The A + C/A/M/A sample exhibited the lowest Rs value, indicating that it had relatively high conductivity. Furthermore, the surface SEM images, shown in Fig. 3e–g and S6, further confirmed the excellent conductivity of the A + C/A/M/A sample; the morphologies of all the samples were almost similar, while the A + C/A/M/A sample appeared brighter under the same accelerating voltage (5 kV). Meanwhile, the cross-sectional SEM images in Fig. S7 clearly show the almost similar thicknesses of A-5, A + C-3/A-2, and A + C/A/M/A samples, with an average thickness of ≈400 nm, which again indicated the positive contribution of 0D and 2D materials to enhancing the electrical properties of composite TCEs.

To further clarify the effects of the different TCEs on the photogenerated charge transport behaviors, steady-state photoluminescence (SSPL) measurements were conducted on the samples based on the glass/perovskite/HTL/TCE (TCE: A-5, A + C-3/A-2, and A + C/A/M/A) configuration. As shown in Fig. 3h, the A + C/A/M/A-coated sample exhibited obvious PL quenching, suggesting that the corresponding TCE has better film quality and contact characteristics, effectively accelerating the charge extraction/transfer, thereby greatly improving the PV output of solar cells [35,36]. Additionally, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the corresponding PH-PSCs further confirmed the remarkable contribution of the A + C/A/M/A composite TCE to the enhancement of charge dynamics in solar cells. The Nyquist curves were fitted based on an electrical equivalent circuit model shown in Fig. 3i, where R S represents the series resistance due to ohmic or electrical contacts, C rec represents the capacitance due to interface charge or ion accumulations, and R rec represents charge transport/recombination resistance [37,38]. Compared with other solar cells, the device based on A + C/A/M/A had the smallest R S (∼29.9 Ω) and the largest R rec (∼9.6 × 103 Ω), which demonstrated that the MoOx/ultrathin Ag films effectively compensate for the shortcomings of the Ag NW TCE, thereby enhancing the collection of photogenerated charges and improving PV performance. Moreover, dark-current measurements were used to reveal the relationship between the different rear TCE structures and PV parameters. As shown in Fig. 3j, the A + C/A/M/A rear TCE with excellent contact and electronic properties prevented the current leakage, thus effectively ensuring efficient PV output of the as-fabricated device [38,39].

Finally, by further optimizing the optical and electrical properties of each component in the A + C/A/M/A composite system, the A + C-2/A-1/MoOx (≈5 nm)/ultrathin Ag (≈2 nm) composite TCE was found to be the best candidate for the preparation of bifacial PH-PSCs. The results of a series of optimizations conducted in this work are presented in Figs. S8 and S9 (Supporting Information). The surface SEM images were first measured for the MoOx/ultrathin Ag composite films with different thicknesses on conventional planar ITO substrates. As shown in Figs. S8a–c, the MoOx/ultrathin Ag film with an appropriate thickness possessed a smoother and denser surface morphology. In contrast, too thin (or too thick) MoOx results in an island distribution of Ag (or Ag clusters) due to insufficient film-forming force (or weakened film-forming force caused by Mo precipitation) [5]. Consequently, the MoOx layer was essential for the formation of a high-quality 2D ultrathin Ag layer, and it was also one of the reasons why MoOx/ultrathin Ag films were used to improve the photoelectric properties of rear TCEs. More importantly, the MoOx film has good antireflection effects in the spectral range of 300–600 nm, which enhances the rear light capture capability of bifacial PH-PSCs [5]. The transmittance and absorption spectra of the ITO, ITO/ultrathin Ag, and ITO/MoOx/ultrathin Ag samples are shown in Figs. S8d and e, respectively. Additionally, the photoelectric properties of each component in the A + C/A/M/A composite configuration were optimized, and the results are shown in Figs. S9a–c, and the A + C-2/A-1/MoOx (≈5 nm)/ultrathin Ag (≈2 nm) sample was found to achieve the best photoelectric balance.

Fig. 4 a presents the J-V test model of the bifacial PH-PSC prepared in this work and its physical photographs. A shadow mask was used during TCE deposition to define the active area of the bifacial PH-PSC, which was 0.2 cm2 for front illumination (from the ITO-glass side) and 0.12 cm2 for rear illumination (TCE side). The J-V curves, external quantum efficiency (EQE), and integrated J SC of the optimized bifacial PH-PSC illuminated on different sides are shown in Fig. 4b and c. Regardless of the incident direction, the corresponding J-V curves were measured at a scan rate of 130 mV s−1 under simulated AM 1.5G one sun illumination of 100 mW cm−2. The detailed PV parameters are summarized in the inset of Fig. 4b. As a result, the maximum PCEs under front and rear illuminations were, respectively, 16.09% and 12.59%, which was relatively high compared with that of the reported Ag NW-based PSCs. Table 1 summarizes the PV parameters, rear electrode configurations, and key techniques of previously reported Ag NW-based PSCs, as well as how our results outperform those of previous studies [1,3,4,15,19,22,24–26,40]. Furthermore, the peak spectral responses were determined from the EQE spectra, which were 87% and 70% under front and rear illuminations, respectively. Thus, excellent photoelectric properties of the A + C/A/M/A rear TCE were further demonstrated. Moreover, the integrated J SCs from the EQE spectra were slightly lower than those of the J SCs measured from the J-V curves. The lower values of the J SC, which are estimated by integrating the EQE spectra, are within a reasonable discrepancy interval (between 10% and 20%) as compared to previously reported values [41].

In addition, the bifacial device exhibited good front and rear PV output stability after continuous illumination for 150 min (∼25 °C, relative humidity <20%). Fig. S10 presents the performance changes as a function of the test time under front and rear illuminations, and the detailed J–V parameters are listed in Table S6. The decline in PV output performance may be attributed to the photodegradation (or thermal degradation) of the interior/interface of each component [42]. Although the ultrathin Ag layer readily forms silver oxide, it is not sufficient to cause the electrical properties degradation of TCEs and devices, as confirmed by previous reports [32,43].

In summary, a novel A + C/A/M/A rear TCE with a 0D/1D/2D composite structure was constructed to achieve ideal photoelectric balance by combining continuous spin coating and thermal evaporation. By systematically optimizing the morphology and photoelectric properties of each component in the composite system, the front and rear efficiencies of the bifacial PH-PSC were greatly improved. Moreover, the effects of the A + C/A/M/A rear TCE on charge dynamics and PV parameters were revealed. We found that 0D C QDs fully filled the voids within the 1D Ag NW network, effectively accelerating the charge transfer/collection rate, thus greatly improving the PV performance of the as-fabricated PH-PSCs. Notably, the Ag NW-coated A + C composite TCEs exhibited higher transmittance than the C QD-covered TCE, which enhanced the backlight harvesting capability of the bifacial devices. Furthermore, the deposition of MoOx/ultrathin Ag films greatly improved the film quality, contact characteristics, and photoelectric properties of rear TCEs, significantly enhancing the PV output of PH-PSCs (especially bifacial devices). More importantly, MoOx was shown to be essential for the photoelectric balance of rear TCEs because it not only promoted the formation of high-quality 2D ultrathin Ag film but also exhibited excellent antireflection effects. Ultimately, the efficiencies of the bifacial PH-PSC based on the A + C/A/M/A rear TCE under front and rear illuminations were, respectively, up to 16.09% and 12.59%, which was superior to that of most reported Ag NW-based PSCs. The present work not only provides a promising direction for the improvement of rear photoelectric balance but also lays a foundation for the broader application of Ag NW-related 0D/1D/2D composite TCEs in efficient bifacial PH-PSCs and other PV devices (e.g., flexible, tandem solar cells).

Deposition of bare Ag NW rear TCEs: A commercial Ag NW dispersion (Nanjing XFNANO Materials Co., Ltd., 1.25 wt%) was ultrasonically diluted to 0.25 wt% with anhydrous IPA (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 99.95%). Afterward, the diluted Ag NW solution (100 μL) was spin coated onto the as-prepared substrates at 1000 rpm for 30 s, and then annealed in air at 60 °C for 5 min to obtain the dried Ag NW network. The number of cycles for Ag NW coating—denoted as A-4 for four cycles, A-5 for five cycles, and A-6 for six cycles—was adjusted to obtain Ag NW rear TCEs with different thicknesses and densities.

Preparation of Ag NW + C QD composite TCEs : First, 5 mg of C QD (Nanjing XFNANO Materials Co., Ltd., 99%) powder was ultrasonically dispersed in 1 mL of IPA to obtain the C QD dispersion; then, the Ag NW/C QD blend solution was prepared by mixing the C QD dispersion with the commercial Ag NW dispersion (1:4 v/v). The as-prepared blend was spin coated onto the substrates at 1000 rpm for 30 s, and the films were dried at 60 °C for 5 min to obtain the Ag NW + C QD composite networks (denoted as A + C CNs). After multiple cycles of A + C coating, the as-diluted Ag NW dispersion (0.25 wt%) was spin coated several times onto the A + C CNs at 1000 rpm for 30 s, and the films were dried under the same conditions. The resulting films were referred to as A-5 (without C QDs), A + C-1/A-4 (one A + C cycle; four Ag NW cycles), A + C-3/A-2 (three A + C cycles; two Ag NW cycles), and A + C-5 (five A + C cycles).

Preparation of Ag NW + C QD/Ag NW/MoOx/ultrathin Ag composite TCEs: MoOx (Aladdin Reagent, ≥ 99.5%) and ultrathin Ag (Aladdin Reagent, ≥ 99.99%) were successively deposited on the as-prepared composite electrodes via thermal evaporation. The thickness of each layer was precisely controlled by setting the parameters of the film thickness in the system. Details of the deposition processes have been described in previous reports [11].

Wanhong Lü: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Lin Fan: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Mingyue Wang: Validation, Investigation, Visualization. Pengfei Wang: Validation, Investigation, Visualization. Qinghua Sun: Software, Data curation. Shuo Yang: Validation, Investigation. Donglai Han: Validation, Investigation. Fengyou Wang: Resources, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Huilian Liu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Jinghai Yang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Lili Yang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

This work has not been published previously, also is not under consideration for publication elsewhere. The coauthors Wanhong Lü, Lin Fan, Mingyue Wang, Pengfei Wang, Qinghua Sun, Shuo Yang, Donglai Han, Fengyou Wang, Huilian Liu, Jinghai Yang, and Lili Yang have approved the submission of this manuscript.
We declare that we have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 61775081, 61904066, 22075101, and 11904127), Program for the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (Item No. 20210509050RQ, 20200801032GH, 20210101410JC, and 20200201022JC), Program for the Science and Technology of Education Department of Jilin Province (Item No. JJKH20210440KJ and JJKH20210611KJ), and the Program of Jilin Provincial Development and Reform Commission (Grant No. 2021C036-3).
